title: Poured in Place
subtitle: A Visitable Fiction Audio Series • Cleveland Dam · North Shore, Vancouver · 1952–1954
description: This is a work of fiction inspired by real places. All characters are fictional. The corruption described in this story is fictional — there is no historical evidence of material fraud on the Cleveland Dam project. The displaced-person labour system it depicts is historically documented.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: The first thing Margaret Aldridge noticed was the noise. It hit her before she’d cleared the gate. A wall of sound coming up from the canyon like something physical. Jackhammers on rock. A concrete mixer grinding. And underneath it all, steady and patient, the river.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: She stood at the fence and looked down. The canyon walls dropped two hundred feet to where the Capilano ran fast and green over boulders the size of cars. Halfway down, a massive wooden framework clung to the rock face like scaffolding for a cathedral that hadn’t been built yet.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: It was April, 1952. She was twenty-eight years old, and she was here to be a supply clerk. She was wearing Gerald’s old work boots because women’s boots didn’t come in a size that fit a construction site. She’d borrowed his rain slicker too. It hung past her wrists. She’d rolled the cuffs twice and they’d already unrolled once on the bus.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: She’d told her husband Gerald she wanted to help with the household finances. The truth was simpler. She’d been alone in their Edgemont Village bungalow for two years, and there was a little less of her sitting at that kitchen table every morning.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Gerald hadn’t wanted her working at his site. She’d insisted. He gave in the way he gave in to everything — politely, with a sigh, already thinking about something else.

script.speaker: Hendricks
script.text: The office manager, Hendricks, walked her through the job. She’d be coordinating deliveries with the concrete crew foreman. A Yugoslav named Zelić. He’s good at his job. He’s not good at conversation.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: On the wall above the filing cabinet was the manifest board. A grid of worker numbers and shift assignments, written in grease pencil on sheet metal. No names. Worker 7, Section 12, 0600. Worker 14, Section 8, 0600. Twenty-three men reduced to digits on a board that could be wiped clean with a rag.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Margaret met Josip Zelić an hour later at the equipment shed. He was tall. Broader than Gerald, with hands that looked carved from the same material he worked with. He looked at her, looked at her clipboard, and said: You’re the new supply clerk.

script.speaker: Margaret
script.text: That’s right.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: You’re also Gerald Aldridge’s wife.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: It wasn’t a question. She felt something close in his expression. A door shutting. Whatever he’d been prepared to give a new clerk, the engineer’s wife was going to get less. He walked her through what he needed. Spoke quickly. Didn’t repeat himself. When he finished, Margaret asked whether batch reports were filed by date or section.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: By section. I just said that.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: He walked away. Her first real mistake came on day three. She transposed two digits on a delivery manifest. Section 14 instead of Section 41. Josip brought it back to her desk. He didn’t raise his voice.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: If the wrong aggregate goes to the wrong section because of a number on your form, the concrete doesn’t meet spec. If the concrete doesn’t meet spec, someone gets hurt. Do it again.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: No please. Two other office workers pretending not to listen. Margaret thought about Gerald, who would never speak to anyone like that. Gerald read people. Gerald adjusted. This man had one setting and it was blunt.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: That was a Thursday. End of Margaret’s second week. The crew was running a major pour on the east formwork. The crack came without warning. A timber brace had split. The platform lurched. Two workers scrambled back. The concrete bucket was still swinging above them — two tons of wet mix over the canyon.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: A third man didn’t scramble. Young. Early twenties. Dark hair. His hands had locked on the railing and his whole body had gone to stone. Josip moved. Not ran. Moved — the way water moves. He went toward the break while everyone else went away. Put his shoulder against the split timber and held the formwork with his body.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: Paolo, move toward me. Now.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: Paolo. Look at my face. Three steps.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Paolo let go. Took one step. The platform shuddered. Took a second step. The timber shifted under Josip’s shoulder with a grinding sound. The formwork tilted. Paolo fell.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Not into the canyon. Sideways, through a gap where a panel hadn’t been secured. Eight feet down onto rebar and wet concrete. Josip couldn’t leave the brace. If he let go, the platform shifted and the men bringing shore timbers were exposed. He held. Forty more seconds. His face turned toward the gap where Paolo had fallen. He could hear the screaming. He couldn’t go to him.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: They brought Paolo up on a stretcher. His left leg was at an angle that legs don’t go. His eyes were open, looking at the sky. Margaret learned the details later. Paolo Ferretti. Twenty-two. From Genoa. Broken femur. Three broken ribs. He wouldn’t be coming back. The site log recorded it as: Workplace injury, formwork section east, one worker transported to hospital. One worker. Not even a name.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: After the ambulance left, Margaret saw Josip walk behind the equipment shed. He stood where he thought nobody could see him. Hands on his knees. Bent forward. His shoulders moving. He stayed like that a long time. Then he straightened. Rolled the shoulder that took the brace. Wiped his face. Picked up his clipboard. Walked back to the formwork.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Josip Zelić had done everything right, and the man fell anyway. And then Josip shook behind a shed and went back to work. Gerald had never moved toward anything dangerous in his life. He was a good man. But he moved toward approval. Toward the next rung. She didn’t hold that against him. Most people don’t do that. But she’d seen it now.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: That evening — the same day as the accident — Margaret was filing batch reports when a number caught her eye. The aggregate spec on a delivery slip didn’t match the engineering requirement. The gravel came from Riverside quarry — the cheap one. The spec called for McNeil — the good one. Someone had sent the wrong material and signed off on it anyway.

script.speaker: Margaret
script.text: I found something.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: I reported the same thing in January. Four deliveries. Wrong quarry. I gave the report to Beaulieu personally. He told me it was handled. Nothing changed.

script.speaker: Margaret
script.text: Someone is falsifying the records.

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: Someone is making money on the difference. And someone is making the paperwork match.

script.speaker: Margaret
script.text: And you let it go?

script.speaker: Josip
script.text: I’m here on a displaced-person visa. Beaulieu is my sponsor. One letter from him and my file gets reviewed. Review means questions. Questions mean deportation. Yugoslavia is not a place I can go back to.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: His locker was open behind him. Pinned inside the door — a photograph. A young woman with dark hair holding a small girl in bright sunlight. Stone wall behind them. The woman smiling. The girl squinting. Margaret saw the photograph. Josip saw her seeing it. His jaw tightened. He reached past her and closed the locker. Neither of them said anything about it.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Margaret took the bus home. Gerald was asleep on the couch. Radio on. She sat at the kitchen table in her work boots and didn’t take them off. The delivery slip. The photograph. Paolo Ferretti on a stretcher, recorded as one worker. Josip’s report, put in a drawer and erased. Josip behind the shed with his hands on his knees.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: After Gerald went to bed, Margaret put on her coat and walked back to the site. The gate guard waved her through. She’d forgotten something in the office. The site was different at night. No machines. No voices. Just the river in the canyon and wind through the formwork. She turned on the desk lamp and pulled the January batch reports. Half of them were missing.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: Margaret stood alone in the trailer, holding a half-empty folder. The lamp made a small circle of light on the desk. Somewhere across the inlet, Paolo Ferretti was lying in a hospital bed. The site log didn’t have his name. Josip Zelić had written a report about bad concrete. The filing cabinet didn’t have that either. Margaret put the folder back. Turned off the lamp. Walked home in the dark. She didn’t sleep. Someone was lying about what was going into this dam. And someone was making the evidence disappear.

characters.name: Narrator
characters.summary: Third-person omniscient. Observes everything, judges nothing.
characters.background: The narrator is the storytelling voice of the series — present in every scene but never a participant. They describe the canyon, the construction site, the weather, and the interior lives of the characters with equal precision. They know more than anyone in the story but never editorialize. The narrator carries the transitions between scenes and the emotional weight of moments that no character can articulate.
traits: Measured and unhurried — lets scenes breathe
Sensory and grounded — describes textures, sounds, smells before emotions
Empathetic but never sentimental
Uses silence as a narrative tool
Occasionally wry, never cynical
characters.motivations: To tell this story honestly. To make the listener feel the canyon wind, hear the jackhammers, and understand why a woman in 1952 would risk everything for a batch report discrepancy.
characters.role: The narrator carries approximately 70% of the audio. They set every scene, describe every action, and bridge the gaps between dialogue. They are the listener’s guide through a world of concrete, rain, and moral compromise.
characters.voice_notes: Warm but restrained. Think a trusted friend telling you something that happened a long time ago — someone who was there, who remembers the details, and who still cares. Pacing should be deliberate, not rushed. Let the periods land. Avoid dramatic vocal performance — the writing does the dramatic work. Read it like you’re sitting across a table from someone, not performing on a stage.

characters.name: Margaret
characters.summary: A restless 28-year-old housewife who takes a clerical job at the dam and discovers both a cover-up and herself.
characters.background: Margaret is 28 and has been married to Gerald for six years. They live in an Edgemont Village bungalow on the North Shore. She studied languages in school — bright, curious, precise — but the postwar domestic contract has slowly been erasing her. She took the supply clerk job ostensibly for household finances. The truth is that sitting at her kitchen table every morning was making her disappear. She has no children. She has a quiet, fierce intelligence that nobody in her life has asked her to use until now.
traits: Observant — notices discrepancies in batch reports and in people
Stubborn in a way that looks quiet from the outside
Honest to a fault, even when honesty is dangerous
Physically brave in moments that surprise her
Carries guilt openly — doesn’t rationalize her choices
Has a dry, understated sense of humour that surfaces under pressure
characters.motivations: Driven by two forces she can’t fully separate: justice and desire. She can’t walk past falsified records — it offends something structural in her. But she also can’t walk away from Josip, and she knows the investigation is tangled up with the attraction. By the end she’s choosing herself — not Josip, not Gerald, but the version of herself that refuses to be erased from the record the way the labourers who built the dam were erased.
characters.role: The protagonist. She discovers the aggregate substitution cover-up, reconstructs the missing batch reports, drafts the anonymous whistleblower letter, and takes the evidence to the district engineer. She falls in love with Josip, loses him to deportation triggered by her own husband, and ends the story standing alone at the dam overlook — unrecorded, unacknowledged, and unbroken.
characters.voice_notes: Clear and direct. She doesn’t perform femininity — she speaks in complete sentences and means what she says. In early episodes she’s guarded, almost clipped. As the story progresses her voice warms but never becomes soft. Even her most vulnerable moments have a backbone. Her anger is cold and precise. Her tenderness is unexpected and real. Avoid breathiness — Margaret is grounded, not fragile.

characters.name: Josip
characters.summary: A Yugoslav displaced-person foreman — blunt, precise, haunted, and quietly heroic.
characters.background: Josip is a concrete foreman in his late thirties who fled Yugoslavia in 1948. He left behind his wife Marta and daughter Ana in Dalmatia. He sends money through a Croatian church network in Vancouver and gets silence in return — he doesn’t know if they’re alive. His right to stay in Canada depends on employer sponsorship from superintendent Beaulieu. One letter to immigration and his file gets reviewed, which means deportation to a country he can’t return to. He built stone walls in Dalmatia for ten years. Good walls. Then the government took the land and the walls didn’t matter.
traits: Blunt to the point of rudeness — has no setting between exact and off
Physically imposing but moves with economy, never waste
Treats danger as a problem to solve, not a performance
Deeply principled — documented every compromised pour even when nobody listened
Emotionally sealed — four years of not feeling things because feeling is dangerous when your life is a document someone else controls
Capable of devastating tenderness when the wall cracks
Protective of his crew — professional, not paternal
characters.motivations: Caught between self-preservation and integrity. He reported the bad concrete in January. His report was filed and ignored. He kept documenting anyway because he can’t not do the right thing, even when it might get him deported. He falls in love with Margaret not because she’s beautiful but because she said ‘that’s the same thing’ when he told her his report was erased — because she sees the world the way he does.
characters.role: The story’s moral centre and its greatest casualty. He first found the concrete problem, held a collapsing timber brace with his body to save his crew, and kept meticulous records of every violation. He falls in love with Margaret on the trail above the dam. Gerald uses Margaret’s own whistleblowing evidence to frame him as a security risk. He’s deported. The photograph of Marta and Ana is the last thing he leaves behind, with instructions for Margaret to send it home.
characters.voice_notes: Deep, steady, accented. His English is fluent but carries the rhythm of Croatian — round vowels, deliberate consonants. He speaks the way he moves: no wasted motion. When he says Margaret’s name it sounds different — softer, rounder, like a word in another language. His anger is quiet and more frightening for it. His tenderness comes through in rhythm changes — when he’s honest about something painful, he slows down because each word costs something. He’s precise, not rough.

characters.name: Hendricks
characters.summary: The site office manager — decent, cautious, and quietly aware of more than he lets on.
characters.background: Hendricks is the office manager of the construction trailer. A thin man who smells like pipe tobacco and speaks like he’s reading from a manual. He’s been at the site longer than most of the engineers and knows where everything is filed — including the things that have gone missing. He’s not corrupt and he’s not brave. He’s a middle-aged man with a government pension who has learned that the safest position is the one where nobody notices you.
traits: Methodical and procedural — everything has a system
Observant but deliberately passive
Dry, understated delivery — ‘That’s just how he is’ is peak Hendricks
Not unkind, but unwilling to take risks for kindness
Knows more than he says about the missing reports
characters.motivations: Wants to survive to retirement without incident. He’s aware that batch reports have gone missing. He’s probably aware of the aggregate substitution. But he has a pension, a routine, and a filing system, and he’s not going to blow any of those up for someone else’s principle. He helps Margaret in small ways — a shrug, a pointed comment — but never enough to implicate himself.
characters.role: The story’s chorus figure. He introduces Margaret to the site, to Josip, and to the filing system that becomes the centre of the investigation. His throwaway observations are some of the most revealing lines in the story. He represents the decent people who see wrongdoing and choose not to act. The story neither condemns nor excuses him.
characters.voice_notes: Flat, matter-of-fact, slightly nasal. Delivers everything at the same temperature — whether explaining the filing system or dropping a line that reframes your understanding of a character. He says important things in the middle of unimportant sentences. Don’t play him for comedy, but let the dryness land. Think of a government clerk who has seen everything and is mildly surprised by nothing. Never rushed.

characters.name: Gerald
characters.summary: Margaret’s husband — charming, hollow, afraid he’s ordinary, and capable of the worst thing anyone does in the story.
characters.background: Gerald is 32, a junior concrete engineer on the dam project. He was once ambitious and tender — funny, present, full of plans. That man has been ground down by the gap between who he thought he’d be and who he is. He lobbied for the dam project because it was the biggest engineering job on the North Shore and he thought it would prove he mattered. It didn’t. He fills out forms about other men’s concrete. He drinks too much rye. He comes home to a house that’s too quiet and falls asleep on the couch with the radio playing to nobody.
traits: Charming on the surface, hollow underneath
Adapts his personality to every room — a social chameleon
Genuinely afraid he’s ordinary, and the fear is eating him alive
Capable of real vulnerability — his porch confession is the most painful scene in the story
Capable of real cruelty — he weaponizes Margaret’s investigation to destroy Josip
Never raises his voice, which makes what he does worse
Leaves without drama — a transfer request, a note that says ‘I hope it was worth it’
characters.motivations: Gerald wants to matter. He wants to be significant. He wants Margaret to look at him the way she did six years ago, and he knows she won’t. When he sees Margaret on the trail with Josip, he doesn’t confront her. He goes to Beaulieu and frames Josip as a security risk using Margaret’s own whistleblowing evidence. It’s clever, it’s legal, and it destroys a man’s life without Gerald ever raising his voice.
characters.role: The story’s most complex antagonist — not because he’s evil but because he’s understandable. His porch confession — drunk on rye at one in the morning, telling Margaret he’s afraid he’s ordinary — makes the audience feel for him. Then he betrays Josip, and both things are true simultaneously. The story doesn’t punish him or redeem him. It lets him leave. His goodbye note — ‘I hope it was worth it’ — is ambiguous even to him.
characters.voice_notes: Smooth, educated, slightly performative. He sounds like a man who is always a little bit rehearsed. His charm is real but practiced — you can hear the effort if you listen carefully. In vulnerable moments the polish drops away and something raw comes through — that’s the real Gerald, and he can’t sustain it. In his cruel moments, he’s terrifyingly calm.
